records.design: Australian consumer health guidance with documented quality process
keyFindings: Symptoms usually peak in 3 to 5 days and improve by day 10.
Suggested home measures include rest, fluids, warm salt-water gargling, avoiding irritants and voice strain.
Pain relief and some OTC throat products may provide symptom relief.
Lists medical and urgent-assessment triggers and Australian support contacts.
limitations: Consumer guidance is not a systematic review and does not provide effect estimates for most supportive measures.
Medicine suitability requires product- and person-specific checking.

records.design: Evidence-based antimicrobial prescribing guideline and evidence summary
records.population: Adults and children with acute sore throat; some self-care evidence adult-only
keyFindings: Most people improve in about one week without antibiotics.
Paracetamol or suitable ibuprofen is recommended for pain or fever.
Medicated lozenges may reduce adult pain by a small amount.
No qualifying evidence was identified for non-medicated lozenges, mouthwashes or local-anaesthetic spray alone.
The committee did not recommend corticosteroids for routine management.
limitations: Guideline is UK-specific and was first published in 2018.
Adult oral-analgesia evidence was low to moderate quality; direct child sore-throat studies were not identified.
Lozenge products and active ingredients vary.

records.population: General public with viral respiratory infection
keyFindings: Antibiotics do not work against viral respiratory infections.
Unnecessary use can cause avoidable adverse effects and antimicrobial resistance.
Antibiotics may be appropriate following clinical assessment when indicated.
limitations: Not specific to all causes of sore throat and not an effectiveness review.

records.design: Cochrane systematic review of nine randomised trials
records.population: 1319 participants older than 3 years; 950 adults and 369 children
keyFindings: Corticosteroids increased complete pain resolution at 24 and 48 hours and shortened mean complete resolution by about 11.6 hours.
Eight of nine trials gave antibiotics to both groups; only one used delayed antibiotics after clinical assessment.
No clear recurrence, absence or adverse-event difference was reported, but adverse-event reporting was poor.
limitations: Mostly adjunct-to-antibiotic evidence and not a direct match for uncomplicated home self-care.
Only two trials included children; rare and recurrent-treatment harms cannot be excluded.
No canonical reader claim was added because applicability and recommendation boundaries require fuller appraisal and clinical review.

records.design: Systematic review and meta-analysis of five controlled trials
records.population: 1772 adults with acute uncomplicated sore throat
keyFindings: Ambroxol 20 mg lozenges produced a small additional three-hour pain reduction versus mint-flavoured lozenges (mean difference -0.11 on the review's repeated verbal-rating outcome).
Benefits beyond three hours were unclear.
limitations: Study reporting quality was low and duplicate publications were identified.
Trial-level sponsorship/conflicts and Australian product applicability remain unresolved.
No canonical reader claim was added.

records.design: Cochrane systematic review update of randomised and quasi-randomised trials
records.population: Children and adults with sore throat; trials span different eras, diagnostic methods and baseline complication risks
keyFindings: Antibiotics reduce persistence of some sore-throat symptoms, but most participants in control groups improve and average symptom benefit is modest.
Some complications were reduced in older trials; absolute contemporary benefit depends strongly on baseline risk and setting.
Adverse effects and antimicrobial resistance must be weighed against benefit.
limitations: Many studies predate current diagnostic practice and contemporary complication rates.
The review does not justify self-diagnosis or self-prescribing; Australian prescribing remains a clinician decision.
Full trial-level funding/conflict extraction was not accessible in this pass.

records.design: Systematic review and meta-analysis of randomised trials
records.population: People aged 5 years or older in emergency and primary-care settings
keyFindings: A single low dose of corticosteroid increased the chance of pain resolution by 24 to 48 hours and shortened time to relief in the pooled clinical-setting evidence.
limitations: Adjunct clinical treatment is not equivalent to unsupervised home self-care.
Rare or recurrent-treatment harms are not resolved by these trials.
No canonical recommendation was added; NICE does not recommend routine corticosteroids for acute sore throat.

records.population: People with acute uncomplicated sore throat in trials of amylmetacresol/2,4-dichlorobenzyl alcohol lozenges
keyFindings: The review reports short-term pain-relief effects for the specific combination formulation.
limitations: Full text, absolute effect extraction and trial-level sponsorship/conflicts were not accessible in this pass.
Findings cannot be transferred to local-anaesthetic, NSAID, ambroxol or non-medicated lozenges.
Australian product equivalence and age restrictions remain unverified; no canonical claim was added.
